testCases.name: test_case_0
testCases.input: Under which constitutional provision has the Public Interest Litigation been filed?
testCases.actualOutput: The Public Interest Litigation (PIL) has been filed under Article 32 of the Constitution of India. This constitutional provision allows individuals to directly approach the Supreme Court of India for the enforcement of their fundamental rights or for the protection of public interests. In this case, the PIL is seeking to protect the rights of road accident victims and their dependents. The issue at hand is a provision in the Motor Vehicles Act that restricts the filing of compensation claims to six months after the accident. The PIL argues that this provision has resulted in the dismissal of numerous claims, defeating the beneficial and welfare objectives of the Act.
testCases.expectedOutput: The Public Interest Litigation has been filed under Article 32 of the Constitution of India.
retrievalContext:
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA  
                                    CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION  
                            WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. _____ OF 2025  
                                  PUBLIC INTEREST LITIGATION  
                (UNDER ARTICLE 32 OF THE CONSTITUTION OF INDIA)  
 
IN THE MATTER OF:  
XYZ ,                                                                                                     …Petitioner  
                                                         VERSUS  
Union of India,  
Through the Secretary,  
Ministry of Road Transport and Highways,                                        …Respondent  
 
To, 
The Hon’ble Chief Justice  of India 
& His Companion  Judges  of this Hon ’ble Court  
 
Humble civil appeal on behalf of the Appellants above -named:   
 
MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:  
1. The present Public Interest Litigation is filed under Article 32 of the Constitution 
of India, seeking to protect the rights of road accide nt victims and their dependents. The Petitioner challenges the constitutional validity of the proviso inserted to sub -
section (3) of Section 166 of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988, by the Motor Vehicles 
(Amendment) Act, 2019, which restricts the filing of com pensation claims before 
the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal to six months  from the date of the accident. 2. The said proviso reads as follows:  
   “No application for compensation shall be entertained unless it is made within 
six months of the occurrence of the accident. ” 
3. The proviso has resulted in the dismissal of numerous claims filed by accident 
victims and their families solely on the grou nd of limitation, thereby defeating the 
beneficial and welfare object of the Motor Vehicles Act. BRIEF FACTS OF THE CASE : 
A. The Petitioner is a social activist working for the welfare and rights of road 
accident victims and their families, involved in pr oviding legal aid and assistance 
to claimants before Motor Accident Claims Tribunals across various States. B. The Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act, 2019 inserted a proviso under Section 
166(3) of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988, effective from 01.04.2022, whic h reads as 
follows:  
“No application for compensation shall be entertained unless it is made within six 
months of the occurrence of the accident. ” 
C. Prior to the amendment, there was no limitation period for filing claims under 
Section 166. This legislative policy ensured that victims were not denied 
compensation due to procedural or financial constraints. D. By reintroducing a strict limitation, the legislature has ignored the socio -
economic conditions of victims, particularly those from weaker s ections, who may 
not be aware of legal procedures, or are incapacitated due to the trauma of the 
accident or loss of earning members. E. Moreover, Section 159 mandates the Police Officer to prepare and submit an 
Accident Information Report to the Claims Tr ibunal within three months, which is 
rarely adhered to. Consequently, victims are unable to file timely claims through no 
fault of their own. F. The impugned proviso has resulted in dismissal of numerous genuine claims 
across the country, thereby defeating  the very object of the Act to provide just and 
fair compensation to road accident victims. 4.

testCases.name: test_case_1
testCases.input: What provision of the Motor Vehicles Act is being challenged in the petition?
testCases.actualOutput: The petition in question is challenging a provision of the Motor Vehicles Act, specifically the proviso of sub-section (3) of Section 166, which was added by the Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act, 2019. This provision is being considered as unconstitutional, ultra vires, and violative of Articles 14 and 21 of the Indian Constitution. This essentially means that the petitioner believes this provision is unfair, beyond the authority of the law-making body, and infringes upon the petitioner's fundamental rights.
testCases.expectedOutput: The petition challenges the constitutional validity of the proviso inserted to Section 166(3) of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 by the Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act, 2019.
retrievalContext:
Penalizing victims for 
administrative delays is manifestly unjust. 5. That proviso creates an unreaso nable classification between victims who can 
afford to file within six months and those who cannot due to socio -economic or 
logistical constraints. The classification bears no rational nexus with the object of 
the Act. 12. INTERIM RELIEF  
Pending disposal of this Petition, the Petitioner prays that this Hon ’ble Court may 
kindly:  
Direct all Motor Accident Claims Tribunals across India to accept and register 
claims filed beyond six months, subject to final outcome of this writ petition. 13. PRAYER  
In light of  the aforesaid , the Petitioner most respectfully prays that this Hon ’ble 
Court may be pleased to:  
a. Issue a writ of Mandamus or any other appropriate writ  declaring th at the 
proviso of sub-section (3) of Section 166 of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 (as 
inserted by the Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act, 2019) as unconstitutional, ultra 
vires, and violative of Articles 14 and 21 of the Constitution of India;  
b. Issue a writ of mandamus or appropriate direction as the nature and 
circumstances of the present case may require;  
AND FOR THIS ACT OF KINDNESS THE APPELANT AS DUTY BOUND 
SHALL EVER PRAY  
FILED BY:  
[Name of Advocate -on-Record]  
Advocate for the Petitioner  
Supreme Court of India  
DRAWN & FILED ON: [Date]  
PLACE:

testCases.name: test_case_2
testCases.input: What limitation period for filing compensation claims is introduced by the amendment?
testCases.actualOutput: The text you provided is a legal document for a case in the Supreme Court of India. The case is about challenging the limitation period for filing compensation claims introduced by the Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act, 2019. This amendment restricts the filing of compensation claims to six months from the date of the accident. However, it is important to note that I cannot provide information on the outcome of the case or any updates beyond the text provided in the document.
testCases.expectedOutput: The amendment introduces a limitation period of six months from the date of the accident to file compensation claims.

testCases.name: test_case_3
testCases.input: Why does the petitioner argue that the six-month limitation is harmful?
testCases.actualOutput: The petitioner argues that the six-month limitation is harmful because it unfairly penalizes victims who cannot file their claims within six months due to socio-economic or logistical constraints. This classification, according to the petitioner, does not have a reasonable connection with the objective of the Act. The petitioner is asking the court to temporarily allow claims filed beyond six months to be accepted and registered while the case is being considered, and ultimately to declare the six-month limitation provision in the Motor Vehicles Act as unconstitutional.
testCases.expectedOutput: The petitioner argues that the six-month limitation leads to dismissal of genuine claims and defeats the welfare objective of the Motor Vehicles Act.

testCases.name: test_case_4
testCases.input: What was the rule regarding limitation before the Motor Vehicles Amendment Act 2019?
testCases.actualOutput: Before the Motor Vehicles Amendment Act 2019, there was a rule that if a person wanted to file a claim for a motor accident, they had to do it within six months. However, the document you provided shows a case where the person is asking the court to declare this rule unconstitutional. In other words, they argue that this rule is unfair because it creates a situation where people who can't afford to file a claim within six months due to socio-economic or logistical reasons are disadvantaged. The person also asks the court to temporarily allow claims to be filed beyond six months, while their case is being considered. If the court agrees with their argument, the rule regarding limitation for motor accident claims could change.
testCases.expectedOutput: Before the amendment there was no limitation period for filing compensation claims under Section 166.

testCases.name: test_case_5
testCases.input: Who is the petitioner described in the case?
testCases.actualOutput: The petitioner in this case is not explicitly named in the provided legal text. However, the document refers to "The Petitioners" in various places, suggesting that there might be more than one individual or entity involved. The petitioners are the ones who have filed this petition under Article 32 of the Constitution of India, as stated in point 11. The petitioners are filing this petition in public interest, as stated in point 6. The specific individuals or entities that make up the petitioners are not disclosed in the text you provided.
testCases.expectedOutput: The petitioner is a social activist working for the welfare and rights of road accident victims and their families.
retrievalContext:
Consequently, victims are unable to file timely claims through no 
fault of their own. F. The impugned proviso has resulted in dismissal of numerous genuine claims 
across the country, thereby defeating  the very object of the Act to provide just and 
fair compensation to road accident victims. 4. Source of Information:  The Petitioner relies on Acts, Amendment Act, Rules 
and Amendment Rules passed by Parliament, published in Official Gazette through 
ordinances by the Central Government, Acts, amendment Acts, Rules and 
Amendment Rules passed by the state governments and p ublished in official 
gazette . 5. Details of Remedies Exhausted: The Petitioners have not approached any court 
of authority for remedy against the issues raised in this Petition. That the Petitioner 
does not have any alternative equally efficacious remedy o ther than to approach 
this Hon'ble Court than by way of filing this writ petition under Article 32 of the 
Constitution of India. 6. Nature of Personal Interest, if any, of the Petitioner:  The Petitioners have no 
personal interest in this Petition and it is  being filed in Public Interest. 7. That the Petitioners have not filed any other writ petition same or similar to the 
present writ petition, neither in this Hon'ble Court nor in any other Court or forum 
across the land. 8. The Petitioner declares that the  issue raised was neither dealt with nor decided 
by a Court of law at the instance of the petitioner or to the best of his knowledge, at 
the instance of any other person. 9. The Petitioners have not approached any authority for relief for the subject 
matte r of this petition. 11. That the Petitioner is filing the instant Writ Petition inter alia on the following 
amongst other grounds:  
GROUNDS   1. That proviso is arbitrary and unreasonable as it creates an inflexible bar to seek 
compensation, treating unequal equally, and disregarding the trauma and 
circumstances of victims. The deletion of limitation in 1994 was a conscious 
legislative choice to remove hardship; its reintroduction is irrational and 
retrogressive. 2. That right to claim compensation for loss of  life or injury is an integral part of 
Article 21. Imposing a six -month limitation, without any provision for condonation 
of delay, effectively denies victims their right to legal remedy and livelihood. 3. The Motor Vehicles Act is a beneficial legislation  intended to protect victims of 
road accidents. Courts have consistently held that such statutes must be interpreted 
liberally in favour of victims. The impugned proviso defeats this legislative intent. 4. That the  Section 159 mandates police to file Accid ent Information Reports 
within three months, delays in compliance mean that many victims receive 
requisite information too late to file within six months. Penalizing victims for 
administrative delays is manifestly unjust. 5. That proviso creates an unreaso nable classification between victims who can 
afford to file within six months and those who cannot due to socio -economic or 
logistical constraints. The classification bears no rational nexus with the object of 
the Act. 12.

testCases.name: test_case_8
testCases.input: Which fundamental rights does the petitioner claim are violated?
testCases.actualOutput: The petitioner in this case claims that their fundamental rights under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution, which includes the right to life and personal liberty, are being violated. Specifically, they argue that a provision in the Motor Vehicles Act imposes a six-month limit for filing compensation claims after a road accident, without providing any provision for delay to be condoned. This, according to the petitioner, effectively denies victims their right to legal remedy and livelihood. They also argue that the provision is arbitrary and unreasonable, and defeats the legislative intent of the Act to protect victims of road accidents.
testCases.expectedOutput: The petitioner claims that the proviso violates Articles 14 and 21 of the Constitution of India.

personal interest in this Petition and it is  being filed in Public Interest. 7. That the Petitioners have not filed any other writ petition same or similar to the 
present writ petition, neither in this Hon'ble Court nor in any other Court or forum 
across the land. 8. The Petitioner declares that the  issue raised was neither dealt with nor decided 
by a Court of law at the instance of the petitioner or to the best of his knowledge, at 
the instance of any other person. 9. The Petitioners have not approached any authority for relief for the subject 
matte r of this petition. 11. That the Petitioner is filing the instant Writ Petition inter alia on the following 
amongst other grounds:  
GROUNDS   1. That proviso is arbitrary and unreasonable as it creates an inflexible bar to seek 
compensation, treating unequal equally, and disregarding the trauma and 
circumstances of victims. The deletion of limitation in 1994 was a conscious 
legislative choice to remove hardship; its reintroduction is irrational and 
retrogressive. 2. That right to claim compensation for loss of  life or injury is an integral part of 
Article 21. Imposing a six -month limitation, without any provision for condonation 
of delay, effectively denies victims their right to legal remedy and livelihood. 3. The Motor Vehicles Act is a beneficial legislation  intended to protect victims of 
road accidents. Courts have consistently held that such statutes must be interpreted 
liberally in favour of victims. The impugned proviso defeats this legislative intent. 4. That the  Section 159 mandates police to file Accid ent Information Reports 
within three months, delays in compliance mean that many victims receive 
requisite information too late to file within six months. Penalizing victims for 
administrative delays is manifestly unjust. 5. That proviso creates an unreaso nable classification between victims who can 
afford to file within six months and those who cannot due to socio -economic or 
logistical constraints. The classification bears no rational nexus with the object of 
the Act. 12.

testCases.name: test_case_9
testCases.input: What interim relief has been requested in the petition?
testCases.actualOutput: The petitioner has asked the court to provide interim relief while their petition is being considered. Specifically, they are requesting that all Motor Accident Claims Tribunals across India accept and register claims filed beyond six months, as a temporary measure, until the court decides on this writ petition.
testCases.expectedOutput: The petitioner requests the court to direct Motor Accident Claims Tribunals across India to accept claims filed beyond six months until the petition is decided.
retrievalContext:
Penalizing victims for 
administrative delays is manifestly unjust. 5. That proviso creates an unreaso nable classification between victims who can 
afford to file within six months and those who cannot due to socio -economic or 
logistical constraints. The classification bears no rational nexus with the object of 
the Act. 12. INTERIM RELIEF  
Pending disposal of this Petition, the Petitioner prays that this Hon ’ble Court may 
kindly:  
Direct all Motor Accident Claims Tribunals across India to accept and register 
claims filed beyond six months, subject to final outcome of this writ petition. 13. PRAYER  
In light of  the aforesaid , the Petitioner most respectfully prays that this Hon ’ble 
Court may be pleased to:  
a. Issue a writ of Mandamus or any other appropriate writ  declaring th at the 
proviso of sub-section (3) of Section 166 of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 (as 
inserted by the Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act, 2019) as unconstitutional, ultra 
vires, and violative of Articles 14 and 21 of the Constitution of India;  
b. Issue a writ of mandamus or appropriate direction as the nature and 
circumstances of the present case may require;  
AND FOR THIS ACT OF KINDNESS THE APPELANT AS DUTY BOUND 
SHALL EVER PRAY  
FILED BY:  
[Name of Advocate -on-Record]  
Advocate for the Petitioner  
Supreme Court of India  
DRAWN & FILED ON: [Date]

testCases.name: test_case_10
testCases.input: Why is the six-month limitation argued to be arbitrary?
testCases.actualOutput: The six-month limitation is considered arbitrary because it creates an unfair distinction between victims who can file their claims within six months and those who cannot due to financial, social, or practical challenges. This distinction doesn't make sense in relation to the purpose of the Motor Vehicles Act, making it unjust for victims who can't meet the six-month deadline. The person filing this petition is asking the Supreme Court of India to either declare this six-month limitation as unconstitutional or issue a directive that extends the deadline for filing claims.
testCases.expectedOutput: It is argued to be arbitrary because it creates an inflexible bar to compensation claims without considering the circumstances or trauma of victims.

testCases.name: test_case_11
testCases.input: What type of legislation is the Motor Vehicles Act considered to be?
testCases.actualOutput: The Motor Vehicles Act is considered to be a legislative act, specifically the Motor Vehicles Act of 1988, as amended in 2019. This act is a set of laws and regulations regarding the use of motor vehicles, such as cars, trucks, and motorcycles, within India. The document provided is a petition asking the Supreme Court of India to declare a specific provision within this act as unconstitutional.
testCases.expectedOutput: The Motor Vehicles Act is considered beneficial legislation intended to protect road accident victims.
